topic.what: Comprehensive pharmacology review of proton pump inhibitors covering acid-dependent activation, CYP2C19 polymorphism effects on metabolism, and PPI-antibiotic synergy in H. pylori eradication
topic.how: Expert-opinion review integrating biochemical, pharmacokinetic, and clinical data on PPI activation in the acidic canaliculus, hepatic metabolism via CYP2C19 and CYP3A4, and the pH-dependent enhancement of antibiotic efficacy against H. pylori
topic.why_matters: Explains inter-individual variability in PPI response through pharmacogenomics and provides the mechanistic rationale for combining PPIs with antibiotics in eradication therapy

characteristics.what_it_measures: Influence of CYP2C19 metaboliser status on PPI plasma levels and acid suppression
characteristics.value: poor metabolisers achieve 3-5x higher AUC and superior acid suppression vs extensive metabolisers
characteristics.vs_baseline: extensive metabolisers may have subtherapeutic acid suppression at standard doses
characteristics.evidence: pharmacokinetic studies and clinical outcome correlations
characteristics.context: Cross-population pharmacogenomic data; frequency of poor metaboliser phenotype varies by ethnicity

novelty: Unified mechanistic account of acid-dependent PPI activation and CYP2C19-driven metabolic variability
Articulated the pharmacological basis for PPI-antibiotic synergy in H. pylori eradication
open_problems: CYP2C19 genotype-guided dosing not yet standard in clinical practice
Whether newer PPIs (e.g. rabeprazole) or potassium-competitive acid blockers overcome CYP2C19 variability
Long-term consequences of profound acid suppression in poor metabolisers require further study
